text: This is an elderly gentleman with a three-time inguinal hernia redo. He had an anatomic repair as a child, then had a direct inguinal hernia repair, and then you can see an indirect plug-and-patch repair done in the past presents with a recurrent hernia.
In the office, he was very symptomatic, chronic groin pain, but had a recurrent bulge. It was difficult to tell if it was coming from the indirect repair or the direct repair in the office.
The surgeon begins dissection in the supravesicular space, extending the flap across to the contralateral side for improved manipulation and visualization.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carrying the flap down to the area directly above the previously placed plug in the recurrent hernia site.
The surgeon encounters sticky tissue medially near the vessels and continues to work laterally, pushing the surrounding fat to protect the nerves.
The inferior epigastric vessels are visible on the right side of the surgical field. Suture material begins to appear in the view, indicating the presence of a recurrent hernia.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to deliver the recurrent hernia, with visible suture material present in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect tissue, applying gentle pressure to allow surrounding contents to separate and fall away, revealing impressive anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a camera angled thirty degrees up, occasionally toggling between thirty degrees up and down for better visualization, as more contents are extracted from the surgical site.
The surgeon navigates below the inguinal ligament, Cooper's ligament, and symphysis pubis, moving back towards the visible plug in the surgical field.
Sorry for the poor quality of this video. Sometimes you get into a groove and you just kind of keep going, forgetting that you're trying to do some video editing afterwards.

The surgeon approaches the mesh overlaying the recurrent inguinal hernia, splitting the flap to access the mesh. The mesh is carefully divided up to the inferior epigastric vessels, with dissection proceeding medially and laterally to minimize stress on the vessels.

The surgeon examines the plug and identifies landmarks including the inferior epigastric vessels and the spermatic cord. The flap is lifted multiple times to ensure visibility, particularly during a redo procedure. Medial dissection is performed to facilitate traction against the mesh for removal.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the corner, with the inferior epigastric vessels visible at the top of the field. The dissection proceeds down to the mesh, leaving a small portion of mesh on the vessels before truncating it. The surgeon works laterally to the vessels, ensuring safety by avoiding nerve entrapment while dissecting down to the posterior surface of the deep internal ring.
The surgeon carefully cuts the flap to reduce torque on the inferior epigastric vessels, improving visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon works with the mesh, ensuring a safe distance from critical structures. They proceed to connect the mesh to the medial dissection area and carefully shave it off.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers on top of the spermatic cord, connecting the dissection points to ensure precise removal of mesh material.

The surgeon carefully dissects the direct space, maneuvering around the corner to access the obturator plug. Attention is given to avoid the nerve on the medial side, which may not be directly on the bone. A pocket is created, and the mesh is sutured into place.

The surgeon places a high medial suture for direct hernia repair, employing a triangular fixation technique with sutures positioned lateral to the chronomotor branch, medial to the iliac vessels, and at high medial and high lateral points.
